skill.name: venue-templates
skill.description: Venue-specific LaTeX templates, formatting requirements, and submission guidelines for journals, conferences, posters, and grants. Use when a target venue imposes strict layout, page limits, anonymization, or agency compliance rules.
skill.summary: Ready-to-use LaTeX templates and formatting guides for academic journals, conferences, posters, and grant proposals.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All critical and high severity static analysis findings are false positives caused by pattern matching on LaTeX mathematical notation and markdown documentation. The 638 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are LaTeX usepackage commands and math expressions, not real cryptographic code. The 'sudo privilege escalation' is a print statement suggesting an install command, not execution. The 'Windows SAM database' is a reference to SAM.gov grants portal. Real risk factors are limited to legitimate subprocess calls (running pdfinfo/pdffonts for PDF validation) and file read/write operations for template customization. These are expected behaviors for a LaTeX template and validation tool.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution via subprocess
medium_findings.description: validate_format.py uses subprocess.run to call pdfinfo (line 59) and pdffonts (line 117) for PDF validation. Commands and their arguments are hardcoded strings, not user-injectable. This is expected and legitimate functionality for a PDF validation tool, but users should be aware of the external tool dependency.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: subprocess.run pattern confirmed with hardcoded arguments to legitimate PDF analysis tools. Arguments are ['pdfinfo', str(pdf_path)] and ['pdffonts', str(pdf_path)] - both hardcoded with no user input injection vector.

medium_findings.title: File Write Operations
medium_findings.description: Scripts write customized template files (customize_template.py:76) and validation reports (validate_format.py:196) to disk. This is expected behavior for a template customization and reporting tool. Output paths are user-specified and the scripts warn before overwriting.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed file write operations that are part of the skill's core functionality. Output paths are user-specified via command-line arguments, not hardcoded malicious destinations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: Reference markdown files contain hardcoded URLs to legitimate academic resources, journal websites, grant portals, and the GitHub repository. These are documentation references, not network exfiltration or malicious connections.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs point to known academic resources (nature.com, neurips.cc, darpa.mil, sam.gov, github.com). Context is documentation and template examples, not malicious payload delivery.

content.user_title: Generate LaTeX Templates for Journals, Conferences, Grants and Posters
content.value_statement: Writing for different academic venues requires hours of formatting work. This skill gives you ready-to-use LaTeX templates and submission guidelines so you can prepare your research document for any journal, conference, grant agency, or poster session instantly.
actual_capabilities: Provides ready-to-use LaTeX templates for Nature, NeurIPS, PLOS ONE, NSF, NIH, and other venues
Queries venue-specific formatting requirements including page limits, margins, citation styles, and anonymization rules
Customizes templates with author names, affiliations, paper titles, and other metadata
Validates compiled PDF documents against venue formatting rules using external PDF analysis tools
Documents writing style conventions and reviewer expectations across different academic venues
limitations: Cannot compile LaTeX files or produce PDF output; requires a local LaTeX installation (TeX Live or MiKTeX)
Template content is illustrative and may become outdated; always verify against the latest official venue guidelines
Validation checks rely on external tools (pdfinfo, pdffonts) and may not detect all formatting issues automatically
Grant templates cover specific agencies (NSF, NIH) but may not include all specialized funding sources

use_cases.title: Journal Paper Submission Preparation
use_cases.description: A researcher preparing a manuscript for Nature or PLOS ONE uses the skill to generate a correctly formatted LaTeX template with proper section structure and citation style.

use_cases.title: Conference Paper Formatting
use_cases.description: A computer scientist writing for NeurIPS gets a template with correct page limits, anonymization requirements, and two-column formatting already configured.

use_cases.title: Grant Proposal Drafting
use_cases.description: A principal investigator uses the skill to set up an NSF or NIH proposal template with agency-specific section requirements and page limits.

prompt_templates.title: Get a Venue Template
prompt_templates.prompt: I need a LaTeX template for submitting to [Nature/NeurIPS/PLOS ONE/NSF]. Give me the template with proper formatting for this venue.

prompt_templates.title: Check Venue Requirements
prompt_templates.prompt: What are the formatting requirements for [NeurIPS/ICML/Nature]? I need page limits, margin sizes, citation style, and anonymization rules.

prompt_templates.title: Validate My Document
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a compiled PDF at [path/to/paper.pdf]. Validate it against [venue name] requirements for page count, margins, and fonts.

prompt_templates.title: Customize Template with Metadata
prompt_templates.prompt: Customize the Nature template with my information: title 'Gene Regulation in Development', authors 'Alice Chen and Bob Wang', affiliation 'Stanford University'. Save the output.
prompt_templates.scenario: Adding author info to a template

output_examples.input: I need a template for submitting to NeurIPS.
output_examples.output:
Here is your NeurIPS LaTeX template:

- Format: Two column
- Page limit: 8 pages plus unlimited references
- Citation style: Numbered [1]
- Anonymization: Required (double-blind)
- Template includes: title section, abstract, introduction, related work, methodology, experiments, conclusion, references

The template is ready for you to fill with your content.

output_examples.input: What are the formatting requirements for an NSF grant proposal?
output_examples.output:
NSF Grant Proposal Requirements:

- Page limit: 15 pages for project description
- Margins: 1 inch all sides
- Font: Times Roman, 11pt minimum
- Line spacing: Single or double
- Key sections: Project Summary, Project Description, Broader Impacts
- Reference format: Follow NSF guidelines

best_practices: Always check the official venue website for the latest formatting guidelines before final submission.
Review templates for placeholder content and replace all example text with your original content.
Run validation checks on your compiled PDF before submission to catch formatting issues early.
anti_patterns: Do not override venue class options, margin settings, or font directives unless the venue explicitly allows it.
Do not submit anonymized manuscripts with author-identifying metadata present in the PDF.
Do not exceed venue page limits expecting exceptions; venue compliance is strictly enforced.

faq.question: Does this skill compile LaTeX files into PDF?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides templates and validation scripts. You need a local LaTeX installation (TeX Live or MiKTeX) to compile PDF files.

faq.question: Are the templates guaranteed to match the latest venue guidelines?
faq.answer: Templates are illustrative and based on common practices. Always verify against the official venue formatting guidelines before submission.

faq.question: What venues are currently supported?
faq.answer: Journals (Nature, PLOS ONE, Cell Press), conferences (NeurIPS, ICML, CVPR, ACL), posters (beamerposter), and grants (NSF, NIH). More venues are documented in the references folder.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for double-blind submissions?
faq.answer: Yes. Templates include anonymization reminders and the validation script can help check for author-identifying metadata.

faq.question: What tools do I need to run the validation script?
faq.answer: You need pdfinfo and pdffonts installed. On Linux install poppler-utils. On macOS run brew install poppler.

faq.question: How do I customize a template for my paper?
faq.answer: Use the customize_template.py script with options for title, authors, affiliations, and email. Run it with --interactive for guided mode.
